The daughters of Zelophehad return to claim what God has already promised them. These five women had appeared before Moses with their petition, grounding their request not in cultural innovation or personal preference, but in the explicit command of the LORD. Now, standing before Joshua and Eleazar, they make the same appeal with the same confidence. Their case is remembered. Their claim is honored. What strikes us is not the novelty of their action—they are not inventing women's rights—but their faithfulness to God's prior word. They know what God has said, and they hold Him to it. Joshua grants their inheritance among their brothers, in keeping with the command of the LORD. This is how God's people inherit their blessings: not by demanding what was never promised, but by trusting what has been spoken.

Yet the chapter moves quickly from this victory of faith to something more troubling. Manasseh receives valuable cities throughout the valleys—Beth-shean, Taanach, Megiddo—but they cannot hold them. The Canaanites were determined to stay in this land. So Israel settles for forced labor, a compromise that leaves the enemy in place. The pattern is familiar by now in Joshua: when God's people lack the will or faith to fully claim what He has given, they make accommodations with evil they should have destroyed. No amount of military advantage overcomes a compromised heart.

When Joseph's sons complain that one portion is too small for their population, Joshua confronts them directly: clear the forest yourself; drive out the Perizzites and Rephaim. They respond with a real obstacle—the iron chariots of the Canaanites in the valleys—and Joshua's answer is severe: you have many people and great strength; you can drive them out. He does not deny their danger. He denies that it should paralyze them. The implicit claim is that God's promise, combined with their actual strength, is sufficient for the task. What they lack is not power but the courage to use it. We live in the same tension: God has given us His Spirit, His Word, and His presence, yet we often settle for less than the full inheritance because the cost of claiming it seems too high. Joshua calls us to something harder than complaint—to the difficult obedience of taking what God has already given.
